{{short description|Basic emotion}}
{{About|the emotion|the character from Inside Out|Disgust (Inside Out){{!}}Disgust {{(--}}''Inside Out'')}}
{{Redirect|Revulsion|the episode of ''Star Trek: Voyager''|Revulsion (Star Trek: Voyager){{!}}Revulsion {{(--}}''Star Trek: Voyager'')}}
{{Redirect|Disgusting|the album|Disgusting (album){{!}}''Disgusting'' (album)}}
[[File:Expression of the Emotions Plate V, 2+3.jpg|thumb|upright=1.3 |[[Oscar Gustave Rejlander]] portraying disgust in plates from Charles Darwin's ''[[The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals]]'']]
{{Emotion}}

'''Disgust''' ({{langx|frm|desgouster}}, from [[Latin]] {{lang|la|gustus}}, {{gloss|taste}}) is an [[emotion]]al response of rejection or revulsion to something potentially contagious<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Badour |first1=Christal L. |last2=Feldner |first2=Matthew T. |title=The Role of Disgust in Posttraumatic Stress |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychopathology |date=July 2018 |volume=9 |issue=3 |article-number=pr.032813 |doi=10.5127/pr.032813 |doi-access=free }}</ref> or something considered offensive, distasteful or unpleasant. In ''[[The Expression of the Emotions in Man and Animals]]'', [[Charles Darwin]] wrote that disgust is a sensation that refers to something revolting. Disgust is experienced primarily in relation to the [[sense]] of [[taste]] (either perceived or imagined), and secondarily to anything which causes a similar feeling by sense of [[Olfaction|smell]], [[Somatosensory system|touch]], or [[Visual perception|vision]]. Musically sensitive people may even be disgusted by the [[cacophony]] of inharmonious sounds. Research has continually proven a relationship between disgust and [[anxiety disorder]]s such as [[arachnophobia]], [[blood-injection-injury type phobia]]s, and contamination fear related [[obsessive–compulsive disorder]] (also known as OCD).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Husted |first1=D.S. |last2=Shapira |first2=N.A. |last3=Goodman |first3=W.K. |title=The neurocircuitry of obsessive–compulsive disorder and disgust |journal=Progress in Neuro-Psychopharmacology and Biological Psychiatry |date=2006 |volume=30 |issue=3 |pages=389–399 |doi=10.1016/j.pnpbp.2005.11.024 |pmid=16443315 |s2cid=20685000 }}</ref><ref name="Cisler2009">{{cite journal |last1=Cisler |first1=Josh M. |last2=Olatunji |first2=Bunmi O. |last3=Lohr |first3=Jeffrey M. |last4=Williams |first4=Nathan L. |title=Attentional bias differences between fear and disgust: Implications for the role of disgust in disgust-related anxiety disorders |journal=Cognition & Emotion |date=June 2009 |volume=23 |issue=4 |pages=675–687 |doi=10.1080/02699930802051599 |pmid=20589224 |pmc=2892866 }}</ref>

Disgust is one of the basic emotions of [[Robert Plutchik]]'s theory of emotions, and has been studied extensively by [[Paul Rozin]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2021/12/27/magazine/disgust-science.html | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211231064128/https://www.nytimes.com/2021/12/27/magazine/disgust-science.html | archive-date=31 December 2021 |date=27 December 2021|work=The New York Times| title=How Disgust Explains Everything| author=Young, Molly }}</ref> It invokes a characteristic facial expression, one of [[Paul Ekman]]'s six universal facial expressions of emotion. Unlike the emotions of [[fear]], [[anger]], and [[sadness]], disgust is associated with a decrease in heart rate (for body-envelope violations) <ref>{{cite book |last1=Rozin |first1=Paul |last2=Haidt |first2=Jonathan |last3=McCauley |first3=Clark |chapter=Disgust |pages=815–834 |editor1-last=Barrett |editor1-first=Lisa Feldman |editor2-last=Lewis |editor2-first=Michael |editor3-last=Haviland-Jones |editor3-first=Jeannette M. |title=Handbook of Emotions |date=2018 |publisher=Guilford Publications |isbn=978-1-4625-3636-8 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Shenhav |first1=Amitai |last2=Mendes |first2=Wendy Berry |date=April 2014 |title=Aiming for the stomach and hitting the heart: Dissociable triggers and sources for disgust reactions. |journal=Emotion |language=en |volume=14 |issue=2 |pages=301–309 |doi=10.1037/a0034644 |issn=1931-1516 |pmc=4050063 |pmid=24219399}}</ref> and proto-nausea of the stomach (for bodily effluvia).<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Alladin |first1=Sameer N. B. |last2=Judson |first2=Ruth |last3=Whittaker |first3=Poppy |last4=Attwood |first4=Angela S. |last5=Dalmaijer |first5=Edwin S. |date=2024-05-01 |title=Review of the gastric physiology of disgust: Proto-nausea as an under-explored facet of the gut–brain axis |journal=Brain and Neuroscience Advances |language=en |volume=8 |article-number=23982128241305890 |doi=10.1177/23982128241305890 |issn=2398-2128 |pmc=11662309 |pmid=39711753}}</ref>

== Evolutionary significance ==
It is believed that the emotion of disgust has evolved as a response to offensive foods that may cause harm to the organism.<ref name="Wicker">{{cite journal |last1=Wicker |first1=Bruno |last2=Keysers |first2=Christian |last3=Plailly |first3=Jane |last4=Royet |first4=Jean-Pierre |last5=Gallese |first5=Vittorio |last6=Rizzolatti |first6=Giacomo |title=Both of Us Disgusted in My Insula |journal=Neuron |date=October 2003 |volume=40 |issue=3 |pages=655–664 |doi=10.1016/S0896-6273(03)00679-2 |pmid=14642287 }}</ref> A common example of this is found in human beings who show disgust reactions to mouldy [[milk]] or contaminated meat.  Disgust appears to be triggered by objects or people who possess attributes that signify disease.<ref name = "Oaten">{{cite journal | last1 = Oaten | first1 = M. | last2 = Stevenson | first2 = R. J. | last3 = Case | first3 = T. I. | year = 2009 | title = Disgust as a Disease-Avoidance Mechanism | journal = Psychological Bulletin | volume = 135 | issue = 2| pages = 303–321 | doi=10.1037/a0014823 | pmid=19254082}}</ref>

Self-report and behavioural studies found that disgust elicitors include:
* body products ([[feces]], [[urine]], [[vomiting|vomit]], sexual fluids, [[saliva]], and [[mucus]]);
* foods ([[Food spoilage|spoiled foods]]);
* animals ([[fleas]], [[ticks]], [[lice]], [[snake]]s, [[cockroaches]], [[worm]]s, [[flies]], [[spiders]] and [[frog]]s);
* [[hygiene]] (visible dirt and "inappropriate" acts [e.g., using an unsterilized surgical instrument]);
* body envelope violations ([[blood]], [[wikt:Special:Search/gore|gore]], and [[mutilation]]);
* [[death]] (dead bodies and organic [[Decomposition|decay]]);
* visible signs of infection<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Curtis |first1=Valerie |last2=Biran |first2=Adam |title=Dirt, Disgust, and Disease: Is Hygiene in Our Genes? |journal=Perspectives in Biology and Medicine |date=December 2001 |volume=44 |issue=1 |pages=17–31 |doi=10.1353/pbm.2001.0001 |pmid=11253302 }}</ref>
[[File:Shannon Marie Morgan searches for her house in Beverly Hills, Florida.jpg|thumb|"When I turned the corner down there and started coming toward the house, I could smell it down there," said Citrus County Sheriff Mike Pendergast about a house overrun with rats. The owner searched for her house after being released from jail,{{failed verification|date=November 2025}} but it had been razed.<ref name="AB2022">{{cite web | title='Horror house' demolished but neighborhood left overrun with rats | website=ABC Action News Tampa Bay (WFTS) | date=20 December 2022 | url=https://www.abcactionnews.com/news/region-citrus-hernando/horror-house-demolished-but-neighborhood-left-overrun-with-rats | access-date=9 April 2023}}</ref>]]
The above-mentioned main disgust stimuli are similar to one another in the sense that they can all potentially transmit infections, and are the most common referenced elicitors of disgust cross-culturally.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Curtis | first1 = Valerie A. | year = 2007 | title = Dirt, disease, and disgust: A natural history of hygiene | journal = Journal of Epidemiology and Community Health | volume = 61 | issue = 8| pages = 660–664 | doi = 10.1136/jech.2007.062380 | pmid = 17630362 | pmc = 2652987 }}</ref> Because of this, disgust is believed to have evolved as a component of a [[behavioral immune system]] in which the body attempts to avoid disease-carrying [[pathogens]] in preference to fighting them after they have entered the body. This behavioral immune system has been found to make sweeping generalizations because "it is more costly to perceive a sick person as healthy than to perceive a healthy person as sick".<ref>{{cite book |first1=Mark |last1=Schaller |first2=Lesley A. |last2=Duncan |chapter=The behavioral immune system: Its evolution and social psychological implications |pages=293–307 |editor1-first=Joseph P. |editor1-last=Forgas |editor2-first=Martie G. |editor2-last=Haselton |editor3-first=William |editor3-last=von Hippel |date=2011 |title=Evolution and the Social Mind |publisher=Psychology Press |doi=10.4324/9780203837788 |isbn=978-1-136-87298-3 }}</ref> Researchers have found that sensitivity to disgust is negatively correlated to aggression because feelings of disgust typically bring about a need to withdraw{{clarify|date=October 2016}} while aggression results in a need to approach.<ref name="Pond2012">{{cite journal | last1 = Pond | first1 = R. S. | last2 = DeWall | first2 = C. N. | last3 = Lambert | first3 = N. M. | last4 = Deckman | first4 = T. | last5 = Bonser | first5 = I. M. | last6 = Fincham | first6 = F. D. | year = 2012 | title = Repulsed by violence: Disgust sensitivity buffers trait, behavioral, and daily aggression | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 102 | issue = 1| pages = 175–188 | doi = 10.1037/a0024296 | pmid = 21707194 }}</ref> This can be explained in terms of each of the types of disgust. For those especially sensitive to moral disgust, they would want to be less aggressive because they want to avoid hurting others. Those especially sensitive to pathogen disgust might be motivated by a desire to avoid the possibility of an open wound on the victim of the aggression. Those sensitive to sexual disgust must have some sexual object present to be especially avoidant of aggression.<ref name="Pond2012" /> Based on these findings, disgust may be used as an emotional tool to decrease aggression in individuals. Disgust may produce specific [[Autonomic nervous system|autonomic]] responses, such as reduced blood pressure, lowered heart-rate and decreased skin conductance along with changes in respiratory behaviour.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ritz |first1=Thomas |last2=Thöns |first2=Miriam |last3=Fahrenkrug |first3=Saskia |last4=Dahme |first4=Bernhard |title=Airways, respiration, and respiratory sinus arrhythmia during picture viewing |journal=Psychophysiology |date=26 August 2005 |volume=42 |issue=5 |pages=568–578 |doi=10.1111/j.1469-8986.2005.00312.x |pmid=16176379 }}</ref>

Research has also found that people who are more sensitive to disgust tend to find their own in-group more attractive and tend to have more negative attitudes toward other groups.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Navarrete |first1=Carlos David |last2=Fessler |first2=Daniel M.T. |title=Disease avoidance and ethnocentrism: the effects of disease vulnerability and disgust sensitivity on intergroup attitudes |journal=Evolution and Human Behavior |date=July 2006 |volume=27 |issue=4 |pages=270–282 |doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2005.12.001 |bibcode=2006EHumB..27..270N }}</ref> This may be explained by assuming that people begin to associate outsiders and foreigners with disease and danger while simultaneously associating health, freedom from disease, and safety with people similar to themselves.

Taking a further look into hygiene, disgust was the strongest predictor of negative attitudes toward obese individuals. A disgust reaction to obese individuals was also connected with views of moral values.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Vartanian |first1=L R |title=Disgust and perceived control in attitudes toward obese people |journal=International Journal of Obesity |date=2 March 2010 |volume=34 |issue=8 |pages=1302–1307 |doi=10.1038/ijo.2010.45 |pmid=20195287 }}</ref>

=== Domains of disgust===
{{See also|Evolution of morality}}
Tybur, et al., outlines three domains of disgust: ''pathogen disgust'', which "motivates the avoidance of infectious microorganisms"; ''sexual disgust'', "which motivates the avoidance of [dangerous] sexual partners and behaviors"; and ''moral disgust'', which motivates people to avoid breaking [[social norms]]. Disgust may have an important role in certain forms of morality.<ref name="Tybur Lieberman Griskevicius et al Microbes, mating, and morality">{{cite journal |last1=Tybur |first1=Joshua M. |last2=Lieberman |first2=Debra |last3=Griskevicius |first3=Vladas |title=Microbes, mating, and morality: Individual differences in three functional domains of disgust |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=2009 |volume=97 |issue=1 |pages=103–122 |doi=10.1037/a0015474 |pmid=19586243 }}</ref>

[[Pathogen avoidance|Pathogen disgust]] arises from a desire to survive and, ultimately, a fear of death. He compares it to a "behavioral immune system" that is the 'first line of defense' against potentially deadly agents such as dead bodies, rotting food, and vomit.<ref name="Tybur Lieberman Griskevicius et al Microbes, mating, and morality"/>

Sexual disgust arises from a desire to avoid "biologically costly mates" and a consideration of the consequences of certain reproductive choices. The two primary considerations are intrinsic quality (e.g., body symmetry, facial attractiveness, etc.) and genetic compatibility (e.g., avoidance of [[inbreeding]] such as the [[incest taboo]]).<ref name="Tybur Lieberman Griskevicius et al Microbes, mating, and morality"/>

Moral disgust "pertains to social transgressions" and may include behaviors such as lying, theft, murder, and rape. Unlike the other two domains, moral disgust "motivates avoidance of social relationships with norm-violating individuals" because those relationships threaten group cohesion.<ref name="Tybur Lieberman Griskevicius et al Microbes, mating, and morality"/>

=== Gender differences ===
Women generally report greater disgust than men, especially regarding sexual disgust or general repulsiveness which have been argued to be consistent with women being more selective regarding sex for evolutionary reasons.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Druschel |first1=B. A. |last2=Sherman |first2=M. F. |title=Disgust sensitivity as a function of the Big Five and gender |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |date=March 1999 |volume=26 |issue=4 |pages=739–748 |doi=10.1016/S0191-8869(98)00196-2 }}</ref>

Sensitivity to disgust rises during pregnancy, along with levels of the hormone [[progesterone]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fleischman |first1=Diana S. |last2=Fessler |first2=Daniel M.T. |title=Progesterone's effects on the psychology of disease avoidance: Support for the compensatory behavioral prophylaxis hypothesis |journal=Hormones and Behavior |date=February 2011 |volume=59 |issue=2 |pages=271–275 |doi=10.1016/j.yhbeh.2010.11.014 |pmid=21134378 |s2cid=27607102 }}</ref> Scientists have conjectured that pregnancy requires the mother to "dial down" her immune system so that the developing embryo won't be attacked. To protect the mother, this lowered immune system is then compensated by a heightened sense of disgust.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Gorman |first1=James |title=Disgust's Evolutionary Role Is Irresistible to Researchers |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2012/01/24/science/disgusts-evolutionary-role-is-irresistible-to-researchers.html |work=The New York Times |date=23 January 2012 }}</ref>

Because disgust is an emotion with physical responses to undesirable or dirty situations, studies have proven there are cardiovascular and respiratory changes while experiencing the emotion of disgust.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Meissner |first1=Karin |last2=Muth |first2=Eric R. |last3=Herbert |first3=Beate M. |title=Bradygastric activity of the stomach predicts disgust sensitivity and perceived disgust intensity |journal=Biological Psychology |date=January 2011 |volume=86 |issue=1 |pages=9–16 |doi=10.1016/j.biopsycho.2010.09.014 |pmid=20888886 }}</ref>

As mentioned earlier, women experience disgust more prominently than men. This is reflected in a study about dental phobia. A dental phobia comes from experiencing disgust when thinking about the dentist and all that entails. 4.6 percent of women compared to 2.7 percent of men find the dentist disgusting.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schienle |first1=Anne |last2=Köchel |first2=Angelika |last3=Leutgeb |first3=Verena |title=Frontal late positivity in dental phobia: A study on gender differences |journal=Biological Psychology |date=December 2011 |volume=88 |issue=2–3 |pages=263–269 |doi=10.1016/j.biopsycho.2011.08.010 |pmid=21889569 }}</ref>

== Non-verbal communication ==
In a series of significant studies by [[Paul Ekman]] in the 1970s, it was discovered that facial expressions of emotion are not [[Emotions and culture|culturally determined]], but universal across human cultures and thus likely to be biological in origin.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Ward |first1=Jamie |title=The Student's Guide to Cognitive Neuroscience |date=2006 |publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=978-1-84169-534-1 }}{{pn|date=September 2024}}</ref> The facial expression of disgust was found to be one of these facial expressions. This characteristic facial expression includes slightly narrowed brows, a curled upper lip, wrinkling of the nose and visible protrusions of the tongue,  although different elicitors may produce different forms of this expression.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rozin |first1=Paul |last2=Lowery |first2=Laura |last3=Ebert |first3=Rhonda |title=Varieties of disgust faces and the structure of disgust |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=1994 |volume=66 |issue=5 |pages=870–881 |doi=10.1037/0022-3514.66.5.870 |pmid=8014832 }}</ref> It was found that the facial expression of disgust is readily recognizable across cultures.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Ekman |first1=Paul |last2=Friesen |first2=Wallace V. |last3=Ellsworth |first3=Phoebe |title=Emotion in the Human Face: Guide-lines for Research and an Integration of Findings |date=1972 |publisher=Pergamon Press |isbn=978-0-08-016643-8 }}{{pn|date=September 2024}}</ref> This facial expression is also produced in blind individuals and is correctly interpreted by deaf individuals.<ref name = "Oaten" /> This evidence indicates an innate biological basis for the expression and recognition of disgust. The recognition of disgust is also important among species as it has been found that when an individual sees a conspecific looking disgusted after tasting a particular food, he or she automatically infers that the food is bad and should not be eaten.<ref name="Wicker" /> This evidence suggests that disgust is experienced and recognized almost universally and strongly implicates its evolutionary significance.

[[Facial feedback hypothesis|Facial feedback]] has also been implicated in the expression of disgust. That is, the making of the facial expression of disgust leads to an increased feeling of disgust. This can occur if the person just wrinkles one's nose without awareness that they are making a disgust expression.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lewis |first1=Michael B. |title=Exploring the positive and negative implications of facial feedback |journal=Emotion |date=August 2012 |volume=12 |issue=4 |pages=852–859 |doi=10.1037/a0029275 |pmid=22866886 }}</ref>

The mirror-neuron matching system found in monkeys and humans is a proposed explanation for such recognition, and shows that our internal representation of actions is triggered during the observation of another's actions.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kohler |first1=Evelyne |last2=Keysers |first2=Christian |last3=Umiltà |first3=M. Alessandra |last4=Fogassi |first4=Leonardo |last5=Gallese |first5=Vittorio |last6=Rizzolatti |first6=Giacomo |title=Hearing Sounds, Understanding Actions: Action Representation in Mirror Neurons |journal=Science |date=2 August 2002 |volume=297 |issue=5582 |pages=846–848 |doi=10.1126/science.1070311 |pmid=12161656 |bibcode=2002Sci...297..846K }}</ref> It has been demonstrated that a similar mechanism may apply to emotions. Seeing someone else's facial emotional expressions triggers the neural activity that would relate to our own experience of the same emotion.<ref name = "Sprengelmeyer">{{cite journal |last1=Sprengelmeyer |first1=R. |last2=Rausch |first2=M. |last3=Eysel |first3=U. T. |last4=Przuntek |first4=H. |title=Neural structures associated with recognition of facial expressions of basic emotions |journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society of London. Series B: Biological Sciences |date=22 October 1998 |volume=265 |issue=1409 |pages=1927–1931 |doi=10.1098/rspb.1998.0522 |pmid=9821359 |pmc=1689486 }}</ref> This points to the universality, as well as survival value of the emotion of disgust.

===Children's reactions to a face showing disgust===
At a very young age, children are able to identify different, basic facial emotions. If a parent makes a negative face and a positive emotional face toward two different toys, a child as young as five months would avoid the toy associated with a negative face. Young children tend to associate a face showing disgust with anger instead of being able to identify the difference. Adults can make the distinction. The age of understanding seems to be around ten years old.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hayes |first1=Catherine J. |last2=Stevenson |first2=Richard J. |last3=Coltheart |first3=Max |title=Production of spontaneous and posed facial expressions in patients with Huntington's disease: Impaired communication of disgust |journal=Cognition & Emotion |date=January 2009 |volume=23 |issue=1 |pages=118–134 |doi=10.1080/02699930801949090 }}</ref>

==Cultural differences==
Because disgust is partially a result of [[social conditioning]], there are differences among different cultures in the objects of disgust. For example, [[Americans]] "are more likely to link feelings of disgust to actions that limit a person's rights or degrade a person's dignity" while [[Japanese people]] "are more likely to link feelings of disgust to actions that frustrate their integration into the social world."<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Haidt |first1=Jonathan |last2=Rozin |first2=Paul |last3=Mccauley |first3=Clark |last4=Imada |first4=Sumio |title=Body, Psyche, and Culture: The Relationship between Disgust and Morality |journal=Psychology and Developing Societies |date=March 1997 |volume=9 |issue=1 |pages=107–131 |doi=10.1177/097133369700900105 }}</ref> Furthermore, practices viewed as acceptable in some cultures may be viewed as disgusting in other cultures. In English, the concept of disgust can apply to both physical and abstract things, but in [[Hindi]] and [[Malayalam]] languages, the concept does not apply to both.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kollareth |first1=D |last2=Russell |first2=JA |title=The English word disgust has no exact translation in Hindi or Malayalam |journal=Cognition & Emotion |date=September 2017 |volume=31 |issue=6 |pages=1169–1180 |doi=10.1080/02699931.2016.1202200 |pmid=27379976 |s2cid=4475125 }}</ref>

Disgust is one of the basic emotions recognizable across multiple cultures and is a response to something revolting, typically involving taste or sight. Though different cultures find different things disgusting, the reaction to the grotesque things remains the same throughout each culture; people and their emotional reactions in the realm of disgust remain the same.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Olatunji |first1=Bunmi O. |last2=Haidt |first2=Jonathan |last3=McKay |first3=Dean |last4=David |first4=Bieke |title=Core, animal reminder, and contamination disgust: Three kinds of disgust with distinct personality, behavioral, physiological, and clinical correlates |journal=Journal of Research in Personality |date=October 2008 |volume=42 |issue=5 |pages=1243–1259 |doi=10.1016/j.jrp.2008.03.009 }}</ref>

==Neural basis==
The scientific attempts to map specific emotions onto underlying neural substrates dates back to the first half of the 20th century. 
[[Functional magnetic resonance imaging|Functional MRI]] experiments have revealed that the [[insular cortex|anterior insula]] in the brain is particularly active when experiencing disgust, when being exposed to offensive tastes, and when viewing facial expressions of disgust.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Phillips |first1=M. L. |last2=Young |first2=A. W. |last3=Senior |first3=C. |last4=Brammer |first4=M. |last5=Andrew |first5=C. |last6=Calder |first6=A. J. |last7=Bullmore |first7=E. T. |last8=Perrett |first8=D. I. |last9=Rowland |first9=D. |last10=Williams |first10=S. C. R. |last11=Gray |first11=J. A. |last12=David |first12=A. S. |title=A specific neural substrate for perceiving facial expressions of disgust |journal=Nature |date=October 1997 |volume=389 |issue=6650 |pages=495–498 |doi=10.1038/39051 |pmid=9333238 |bibcode=1997Natur.389..495P }}</ref>  The research has supported that there are independent neural systems in the brain, each handling a specific basic emotion.<ref name="Wicker" /> Specifically, f-MRI studies have provided evidence for the activation of the insula in disgust recognition, as well as visceral changes in disgust reactions such as the feeling of nausea.<ref name="Wicker" /> The importance of disgust recognition and the visceral reaction of "feeling disgusted" is evident when considering the survival of organisms, and the evolutionary benefit of avoiding contamination.<ref name="Wicker" />

===Insula===
[[File:Gray731.png|thumb|The insula of the left side, exposed by removing the opercula. From [[Henry Gray]], [[Warren Harmon Lewis]] (1918). ''[[Gray's Anatomy|Anatomy of the Human Body]]''. Fig. 731]]
The '''insula''' (or [[insular cortex]]), is the main neural structure involved in the emotion of disgust.<ref name="Wicker" /><ref name="Sprengelmeyer" /><ref name = "Calder">{{cite journal | last1 = Calder | year = 2000 | title = Impaired recognition and experience of disgust following brain injury | journal = Nature Neuroscience | volume = 3 | issue = 11| pages = 1077–1088 | display-authors = 1 | doi = 10.1038/80586 | first1 = Andrew J. | last2 = Keane | first2 = Jill | last3 = Manes | first3 = Facundo | last4 = Antoun | first4 = Nagui | last5 = Young | first5 = Andrew W. | pmid=11036262| s2cid = 40182662 }}</ref> The insula has been shown by several studies to be the main neural correlate of the feeling of disgust both in humans and in macaque monkeys. The insula is activated by unpleasant tastes, smells, and the visual recognition of disgust in [[conspecific]] organisms.<ref name="Wicker" />

The '''anterior insula''' is an [[olfactory]] and [[gustatory]] center that controls [[visceral]] sensations and the related autonomic responses.<ref name="Wicker" /> It also receives visual information from the anterior portion of the ventral superior temporal cortex, where cells have been found to respond to the sight of faces.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Keysers | first1 = C. | last2 = Xiao | first2 = D. K. | last3 = Foldiak | first3 = P. | last4 = Perrett | first4 = D. I. | year = 2001 | title = The speed of sight | journal = Cognitive Neuroscience | volume = 13 | issue = 1| pages = 90–101 | doi = 10.1162/089892901564199 | pmid = 11224911 | s2cid = 9433619 }}</ref>

The '''posterior insula''' is characterized by connections with [[Auditory system|auditory]], [[somatosensory]], and [[premotor]] areas, and is not related to the olfactory or gustatory modalities.<ref name="Wicker" />

The fact that the insula is necessary for our ability to feel and recognize the emotion of disgust is further supported by neuropsychological studies. Both Calder (2000) and Adolphs (2003) showed that lesions on the anterior insula lead to deficits in the experience of disgust and recognizing facial expressions of disgust in others.<ref name = "Calder" /><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Adolphs | year = 2003 | title = Dissociable neural systems for recognizing emotions | journal = Brain and Cognition | volume = 52 | issue = 1| pages = 61–69 | display-authors = 1 | doi = 10.1016/S0278-2626(03)00009-5 | pmid = 12812805 | first1 = Ralph | last2 = Tranel | first2 = Daniel | last3 = Damasio | first3 = Antonio R. | s2cid = 25826623 }}</ref> The patients also reported having reduced sensations of disgust themselves. Furthermore, electrical stimulation of the anterior insula conducted during neurosurgery triggered nausea, the feeling of wanting to throw up and uneasiness in the stomach. Finally, electrically stimulating the anterior insula through implanted electrodes produced sensations in the throat and mouth that were "difficult to stand".<ref name="Wicker" /> These findings demonstrate the role of the insula in transforming unpleasant sensory input into physiological reactions, and the associated feeling of disgust.<ref name="Wicker" />

Studies have demonstrated that the insula is activated by disgusting stimuli, and that observing someone else's facial expression of disgust seems to automatically retrieve a neural representation of disgust.<ref name="Wicker" /><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Stark | first1 = R. | last2 = Zimmermann | first2 = M. | last3 = Kagerer | first3 = S. | last4 = Schienle | first4 = A. | last5 = Walter | first5 = B. | year = 2007 | title = Hemodynamic brain correlates of disgust and fear ratings | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 37 | issue = 2| pages = 663–673 | doi=10.1016/j.neuroimage.2007.05.005| pmid = 17574869 | s2cid = 3355457 }}</ref> Furthermore, these findings emphasize the role of the insula in feelings of disgust.

One particular neuropsychological study focused on patient NK who was diagnosed with a left hemisphere infarction involving the insula, internal
capsule, putamen and globus pallidus. NK's neural damage included the insula and [[putamen]] and it was found that NK's overall response to disgust-inducing stimuli was significantly lower than that of controls.<ref name = "Calder" /> The patient showed a reduction in disgust-response on eight categories including food, animals, body products, envelope violation and death.<ref name = "Calder" /> Moreover, NK incorrectly categorized disgust facial expressions as anger. The results of this study support the idea that NK had damage to a system involved in recognizing social signals of disgust, due to a damaged insula caused by neurodegeneration.<ref name = "Calder" />

==Disorders==

===Huntington's disease===
Many patients with [[Huntington's disease]], a genetically transmitted progressive neurodegenerative disease, are unable to recognize expressions of disgust in others and also don't show reactions of disgust to foul odors or tastes.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Mitchell |first1=I. J. |title=Huntington's Disease Patients Show Impaired Perception of Disgust in the Gustatory and Olfactory Modalities |journal=Journal of Neuropsychiatry |date=February 2005 |volume=17 |issue=1 |pages=119–121 |doi=10.1176/appi.neuropsych.17.1.119 }}</ref> The inability to recognize expressions of disgust appears in carriers of the Huntington gene before other symptoms appear.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sprengelmeyer |first1=R. |last2=Schroeder |first2=U. |last3=Young |first3=A.W. |last4=Epplen |first4=J.T. |title=Disgust in pre-clinical Huntington's disease: A longitudinal study |journal=Neuropsychologia |date=January 2006 |volume=44 |issue=4 |pages=518–533 |doi=10.1016/j.neuropsychologia.2005.07.003 |pmid=16098998 }}</ref> People with Huntington's disease are impaired at recognition of anger and fear, and experience a notably severe problem with disgust recognition.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sprengelmeyer |first1=Reiner |last2=Young |first2=Andrew W. |last3=Calder |first3=Andrew J. |last4=Karnat |first4=Anke |last5=Lange |first5=Herwig |last6=Hömberg |first6=Volker |last7=Perrett |first7=David I. |last8=Rowland |first8=Duncan |title=Loss of disgust |journal=Brain |date=1996 |volume=119 |issue=5 |pages=1647–1665 |doi=10.1093/brain/119.5.1647 |pmid=8931587 }}</ref>

===Major depressive disorder===
Patients with [[Major depressive disorder|major depression]] have been found to display greater brain activation to facial expressions of disgust.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Surguladze |first1=Simon A. |last2=El-Hage |first2=Wissam |last3=Dalgleish |first3=Tim |last4=Radua |first4=Joaquim |last5=Gohier |first5=Benedicte |last6=Phillips |first6=Mary L. |title=Depression is associated with increased sensitivity to signals of disgust: A functional magnetic resonance imaging study |journal=Journal of Psychiatric Research |date=October 2010 |volume=44 |issue=14 |pages=894–902 |doi=10.1016/j.jpsychires.2010.02.010 |pmid=20307892 |pmc=4282743 }}</ref> Self-disgust, which is disgust directed towards one's own actions, may also contribute to the relationship between dysfunctional thoughts and depression.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Simpson |first1=J. |last2=Hillman |first2=R. |last3=Crawford |first3=T. |last4=Overton |first4=P. G. |title=Self-esteem and self-disgust both mediate the relationship between dysfunctional cognitions and depressive symptoms |journal=Motivation and Emotion |date=23 October 2010 |volume=34 |issue=4 |pages=399–406 |doi=10.1007/s11031-010-9189-2 }}</ref>

=== Obsessive-compulsive disorder ===
The emotion of disgust may have an important role in understanding the neurobiology of [[obsessive-compulsive disorder]] (OCD), particularly in those with contamination preoccupations.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Stein | first1 = D.J. | last2 = Liu | first2 = Y. | last3 = Shapira | first3 = N.A. | last4 = Goodman | first4 = W.K. | year = 2001 | title = The psychobiology of obsessive-compulsive disorder: How important is disgust? | journal = Current Psychiatry Reports | volume = 3 | issue = 4| pages = 281–287 | doi = 10.1007/s11920-001-0020-3 | pmid = 11470034 | s2cid = 11828609 }}</ref> In a study by Shapira & colleagues (2003), eight OCD subjects with contamination preoccupations and eight healthy volunteers viewed pictures from the International Affective Picture System during f-MRI scans. OCD subjects showed significantly greater neural responses to disgust-invoking images, specifically in the right insula.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Shapira |first1=Nathan A. |last2=Liu |first2=Yijun |last3=He |first3=Alex G. |last4=Bradley |first4=Margaret M. |last5=Lessig |first5=Mary C. |last6=James |first6=George A. |last7=Stein |first7=Dan J. |last8=Lang |first8=Peter J. |last9=Goodman |first9=Wayne K. |title=Brain activation by disgust-inducing pictures in obsessive-compulsive disorder |journal=Biological Psychiatry |date=October 2003 |volume=54 |issue=7 |pages=751–756 |doi=10.1016/s0006-3223(03)00003-9 |pmid=14512216 }}</ref> Furthermore, Sprengelmeyer (1997) found that the brain activation associated with disgust included the insula and part of the gustatory cortex that processes unpleasant tastes and smells. OCD subjects and healthy volunteers showed activation patterns in response to disgust pictures that differed significantly at the right insula. In contrast, the two groups were similar in their response to threat-inducing pictures, with no significant group differences at any site.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Sprengelmeyer | first1 = R. | last2 = Young | first2 = A. W. | last3 = Pundt | first3 = I. | last4 = Sprengelmeyer | first4 = A. | last5 = Calder | first5 = A. J. | last6 = Berrios | first6 = G. | last7 = Winkel | first7 = R. | last8 = Vollmoeller | first8 = W. | last9 = Kuhn | first9 = W. | last10 = Sartory | first10 = G. | last11 = Przuntek | first11 = H. | year = 1997 | title = Disgust implicated in obsessive-compulsive disorder | journal = Biological Sciences | volume = 264 | issue = 1389| pages = 1767–1773|pmid=9447734 | doi=10.1098/rspb.1997.0245 | pmc=1688750| bibcode = 1997RSPSB.264.1767S }}</ref>

== Animal research ==
With respect to studies using [[rat]]s, prior research of signs of a conditioned disgust response have been experimentally verified by Grill and Norgren (1978) who developed a systematic test to assess [[palatability]]. The Taste Reactivity (TR) test has thus become a standard tool in measuring disgust response.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Grill | first1 = H.C. | last2 = Norgren | first2 = R. | year = 1978a | title = The taste Reactivity Test. I: Miimetic responses to gustatory stimuli in neurologically normal rats | journal = Brain Research | volume = 143 | issue = 2| pages = 263–279 | doi = 10.1016/0006-8993(78)90568-1 | pmid = 630409 | s2cid = 4637907 }}</ref> When given a stimulus intraorally which had been previously paired with a [[nausea]]-inducing substance, rats will show conditioned disgust reactions. "Gaping" in rats is the most dominant conditioned disgust reaction and the muscles used in this response mimic those used in species capable of vomiting.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Travers | first1 = J. B. | last2 = Norgren | first2 = R. | year = 1986 | title = Electromyographic analysis of the ingestion and rejection of sapid stimuli in the rat | journal = Behavioral Neuroscience | volume = 100 | issue = 4| pages = 544–555 | doi = 10.1037/0735-7044.100.4.544 | pmid = 3741605 }}</ref> Studies have shown that treatments that reduced [[serotonin]] availability or that activate the [[endocannabinoid system]] can interfere with the expression of a conditioned disgust reaction in rats. These researchers showed that as nausea produced conditioned disgust reactions, by administering the rats with an antinausea treatment they could prevent toxin-induced conditioned disgust reactions. Furthermore, in looking at the different disgust and vomiting reactions between rats and shrews the authors showed that these reactions (particularly vomiting) play a crucial role in the associative processes that govern food selection across species.<ref name="Parker Rana Limebeer Conditioned nausea in rats">{{cite journal |last1=Parker |first1=Linda A. |last2=Rana |first2=Shadna A. |last3=Limebeer |first3=Cheryl L. |title=Conditioned nausea in rats: Assessment by conditioned disgust reactions, rather than conditioned taste avoidance |journal=Canadian Journal of Experimental Psychology |date=September 2008 |volume=62 |issue=3 |pages=198–209 |doi=10.1037/a0012531 |pmid=18778149 }}</ref>

In discussing specific neural locations of disgust, research has shown that forebrain mechanisms are necessary for rats to acquire conditioned disgust for a specific emetic (vomit-inducing) substance (such as [[lithium chloride]]).<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Grill | first1 = H.C. | last2 = Norgren | first2 = R. | year = 1978b | title = Chronically decerebrate rats demonstrate satiation but not bait shyness | journal = Science | volume = 201 | issue = 4352| pages = 267–269 | doi = 10.1126/science.663655 | pmid = 663655 | bibcode = 1978Sci...201..267G }}</ref> Other studies have shown that lesions to the area postrema<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Eckel |first1=Lisa A. |last2=Ossenkopp |first2=Klaus-Peter |title=Area postrema mediates the formation of rapid, conditioned palatability shifts in lithium-treated rats |journal=Behavioral Neuroscience |date=February 1996 |volume=110 |issue=1 |pages=202–212 |doi=10.1037/0735-7044.110.1.202 |pmid=8652067 }}</ref> and the parabrachial nucleus of the [[pons]]<ref name="Flynn, F. W 1991">{{cite journal | last1 = Flynn | first1 = F. W | last2 = Grill | first2 = H. J. | last3 = Shulkin | first3 = J. | last4 = Norgren | first4 = R. | year = 1991 | title = Central gustatory lesions: II. Effects on sodium appetite, taste aversion learning, and feeding behaviors | journal = Behavioral Neuroscience | volume = 105 | issue = 6| pages = 944–954 | doi = 10.1037/0735-7044.105.6.944 | pmid = 1777107 }}</ref> but not the nucleus of the solitary tract<ref name="Flynn, F. W 1991"/> prevented conditioned disgust. Moreover, lesions of the [[Dorsum (biology)|dorsal]] and [[Anatomical terms of location#Left and right (lateral), and medial|medial]] [[raphe nuclei]] (depleting forebrain [[serotonin]]) prevented the establishment of lithium chloride-induced conditioned disgust.<ref name="Parker Rana Limebeer Conditioned nausea in rats"/>

== Non-human primates ==
Non-human primates display signs of disgust and aversion to biological contaminants. Exposure to bodily excrements that usually elicit disgust reactions in humans, such as feces, semen, or blood, have an impact on primates' feeding preferences.<ref name=":2">{{cite journal |last1=Case |first1=Trevor I. |last2=Stevenson |first2=Richard J. |last3=Byrne |first3=Richard W. |last4=Hobaiter |first4=Catherine |title=The animal origins of disgust: Reports of basic disgust in nonhuman great apes |journal=Evolutionary Behavioral Sciences |date=July 2020 |volume=14 |issue=3 |pages=231–260 |id={{ProQuest|2229333470}} |doi=10.1037/ebs0000175 |hdl=10023/17757 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Chimpanzees generally avoid the smells of biological contaminants, but only show a weak tendency to move away from these odors, possibly because olfactory stimuli are not enough to give chimps a high enough threat level to move away.<ref name=":3">{{cite journal |last1=Sarabian |first1=Cecile |last2=Ngoubangoye |first2=Barthelemy |last3=MacIntosh |first3=Andrew J. J. |title=Avoidance of biological contaminants through sight, smell and touch in chimpanzees |journal=Royal Society Open Science |date=November 2017 |volume=4 |issue=11 |article-number=170968 |doi=10.1098/rsos.170968  |doi-access=free|pmid=29291090 |pmc=5717664 }}</ref> Chimpanzees physically recoil when presented with food items on soft, moist substrates, possibly because in nature, moisture, softness, and warmth are characteristics needed to grow pathogens.<ref name=":3" /> These responses are functionally similar to what humans' responses would be to the same kinds of stimuli, indicating that the underlying mechanism for this behavior is similar to ours.<ref name=":4">{{cite thesis |doi=10.14989/doctor.k21615 |hdl=2433/242653 |date=2019 |last1=Cecile |first1=Anna Sarabian |title=Exploring the origins of disgust: Evolution of parasite avoidance behaviors in primates |publisher=Kyoto University }}{{pn|date=September 2024}}</ref>

Chimpanzees generally avoid food contaminated with dirt or feces, but most individuals still consume these kinds of contaminated foods.<ref name=":2" /> While chimps do show a preference for food items with lower contamination risk, they do not avoid risk altogether, as most humans would. This may be due to a trade-off between the nutritional value of the food items and the risk of infection from the biological contaminants, with the chimps weighing the benefit of the food more heavily than the risk of contamination.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Rottman |first1=Joshua |title=Evolution, Development, and the Emergence of Disgust |journal=Evolutionary Psychology |date=April 2014 |volume=12 |issue=2 |pages=417–433 |doi=10.1177/147470491401200209 |pmid=25299887 |pmc=10480999 }}</ref> In contrast to chimpanzees, Japanese macaques are more sensitive to visual cues of contaminants when there is no accompanying odor.<ref name=":4" /> Bonobos are most sensitive to fecal odors and rotten food odors.<ref name="Sarabian 20170195">{{cite journal |last1=Sarabian |first1=Cecile |last2=Belais |first2=Raphael |last3=MacIntosh |first3=Andrew J. J. |title=Feeding decisions under contamination risk in bonobos |journal=Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences |date=4 June 2018 |volume=373 |issue=1751 |article-number=20170195 |doi=10.1098/rstb.2017.0195 |pmid=29866924 |pmc=6000142 }}</ref> Overall, primates incorporate various senses in their feeding decisions, with disgust being an adaptive trait that helps them avoid potential parasites and other threats from contaminants.

The most frequently reported disgust-like behavior in non-human primates is expelling bad-tasting food items, but even this behavior is not very common. This might be because primates effectively avoid potentially bad-tasting food items, and food that is avoided cannot be expelled, hence the low observation rate of this behavior.<ref name=":2" /> Primates, notably gorillas and chimpanzees, occasionally make facial expressions such as grimacing and tongue protrusions after having bad-tasting food.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Whiten |first1=A. |last2=Goodall |first2=J. |last3=McGrew |first3=W. C. |last4=Nishida |first4=T. |last5=Reynolds |first5=V. |last6=Sugiyama |first6=Y. |last7=Tutin |first7=C. E. G. |last8=Wrangham |first8=R. W. |last9=Boesch |first9=C. |title=Cultures in chimpanzees |journal=Nature |date=June 1999 |volume=399 |issue=6737 |pages=682–685 |doi=10.1038/21415 |pmid=10385119 |bibcode=1999Natur.399..682W |s2cid=4385871 }}</ref> Individual primate preferences vary widely, some tolerating extremely bitter food, while others are more particular.<ref name=":4" /> Taste preferences are more often noticed in high ranking individuals, likely because lower ranked individuals may have to tolerate less-desired foods.<ref name=":2" />

While in humans there is a strong difference in disgust reactions between the two sexes, this difference has not been documented in non-human primates. In humans, women generally report greater disgust than men.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Haidt |first1=Jonathan |last2=McCauley |first2=Clark |last3=Rozin |first3=Paul |title=Individual differences in sensitivity to disgust: A scale sampling seven domains of disgust elicitors |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |date=May 1994 |volume=16 |issue=5 |pages=701–713 |doi=10.1016/0191-8869(94)90212-7 }}</ref> In bonobos and chimps, females are not any more avoidant than males of contamination risk.<ref name="Sarabian 20170195"/> There is some evidence suggesting that juveniles are less contamination-risk avoidant than adults, which is in line with research on the development of the disgust response in humans.<ref name=":2" />

Coprophagy is commonly observed in chimpanzees, possibly suggesting that chimps do not really have a disgust mechanism the way humans do.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Krief |first1=Sabrina |last2=Jamart |first2=Aliette |last3=Hladik |first3=Claude-Marcel |title=On the possible adaptive value of coprophagy in free-ranging chimpanzees |journal=Primates |date=April 2004 |volume=45 |issue=2 |pages=141–145 |doi=10.1007/s10329-003-0074-4 |pmid=14986147 }}</ref> Coprophagy is usually only done to re-ingest seeds from one's own feces, which is less risky than ingesting others' feces in terms of exposure to new parasites.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Bertolani |first1=Paco |last2=Pruetz |first2=Jill D. |title=Seed Reingestion in Savannah Chimpanzees (Pan troglodytes verus) at Fongoli, Senegal |journal=International Journal of Primatology |date=12 July 2011 |volume=32 |issue=5 |pages=1123–1132 |doi=10.1007/s10764-011-9528-5 }}</ref> Additionally, chimps often use leaves and twigs to wipe themselves when they stepped in others' feces instead of removing it with their bare hands.<ref name=":2" /> Great apes almost always remove feces from their bodies after accidentally stepping in it, even in instances where it would be beneficial to wait. For example, when grapes are being passed out to chimps and they accidentally step in feces, they almost always take the time to stop and wipe it off even if it means missing out on food.<ref name=":4" />

Unlike in humans, the avoidance of social contamination (ex: staying away from sick conspecifics) is rare in great apes.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hart |first1=Benjamin L. |last2=Hart |first2=Lynette A. |title=How mammals stay healthy in nature: the evolution of behaviours to avoid parasites and pathogens |journal=Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences |date=4 June 2018 |volume=373 |issue=1751 |article-number=20170205 |doi=10.1098/rstb.2017.0205 |pmid=29866918 |pmc=6000140 }}</ref> Instead, great apes often groom sick conspecifics or just treat them with indifference.<ref name=":2" /> Additionally, great apes treat products of a sick conspecific such as mucus or blood with interest or indifference.<ref name=":4" /> This is in contrast with human disease avoidance where avoiding those who appear sick is a key feature.

Taken together, studies on the disgust reaction in primates show that disgust is adaptive in primates and that the avoidance of potential sources of pathogens is triggered by the same contaminants as for humans.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Curtis |first1=Valerie |last2=de Barra |first2=Mícheál |last3=Aunger |first3=Robert |title=Disgust as an adaptive system for disease avoidance behaviour |journal=Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences |date=12 February 2011 |volume=366 |issue=1563 |pages=389–401 |doi=10.1098/rstb.2010.0117 |pmid=21199843 |pmc=3013466 }}</ref> The adaptive problems that primates faced did not align to the degree that they did for early humans, which is why disgust manifests differently in humans and non-human primates.<ref name=":5">{{cite book |last1=Kelly |first1=Daniel |title=Yuck!: The Nature and Moral Significance of Disgust |date=2011 |publisher=MIT Press |isbn=978-0-262-29484-3 }}{{pn|date=September 2024}}</ref> Differences in disgust responses between humans and non-human primates likely reflects their unique ecological standpoints. Rather than disgust being a unique human emotion, disgust is a continuation of the parasite and infection avoidance behavior found in all animals.<ref name=":2" /> One theory explaining the difference is that since primates are largely foragers and never shifted to the hunter-scavenger lifestyle with a diet high in meat, they were never exposed to the new wave of pathogens that humans were exposed to, as well as the selection pressures that would come with this diet. Therefore, the disgust mechanisms in primates remained muted, only strong enough to address the distinct problems primates faced in their evolutionary history.<ref name=":5" /> Additionally, disgust-like behavior in great apes should be lower than in humans because they live in less hygienic conditions. Humans' clean habits over generations has reduced how frequently we are exposed to disgust elicitors and has likely expanded the stimuli that would elicit disgust reactions in us. Great apes on the other hand are constantly exposed to disgust elicitors, leading to habituation and a muted form of disgust compared to modern humans.<ref name=":4" />

==Morality==
{{See also|Moral emotions|Social intuitionism}}
Although disgust was first thought to be a motivation for humans to only physical contaminants, it has since been applied to moral and social moral contaminants as well. The similarities between these types of disgust can especially be seen in the way people react to the contaminants. For example, if someone stumbles upon a pool of vomit, they will do whatever possible to place as much distance between themselves and the vomit as possible, which can include pinching the nose, closing the eyes, or running away. Likewise, when a group experiences someone who cheats, rapes, or murders another member of the group, its reaction is to shun or expel that person from the group.<ref name="Jones Fitness Moral hypervigilance">{{cite journal |last1=Jones |first1=Andrew |last2=Fitness |first2=Julie |title=Moral hypervigilance: The influence of disgust sensitivity in the moral domain. |journal=Emotion |date=2008 |volume=8 |issue=5 |pages=613–627 |doi=10.1037/a0013435 |pmid=18837611 }}</ref>

Arguably, there is a completely different construct of the emotion of disgust from the core disgust that can be seen in Ekman's basic emotions. Socio-moral disgust occurs when social or moral boundaries appear to be violated, the socio-moral aspect centers on human violations of the autonomy and dignity of others (e.g., racism, hypocrisy, disloyalty).<ref name=":0">{{cite journal |last1=Simpson |first1=Jane |last2=Carter |first2=Sarah |last3=Anthony |first3=Susan H. |last4=Overton |first4=Paul G. |title=Is Disgust a Homogeneous Emotion? |journal=Motivation and Emotion |date=March 2006 |volume=30 |issue=1 |pages=31–41 |doi=10.1007/s11031-006-9005-1 }}</ref> Socio-moral disgust is different from core disgust. In the 2006 study done by Simpson and colleagues, there was a divergence found in disgust responses between the core elicitors of disgust and the socio-moral elicitors of disgust, suggesting that the makeup of core and socio-moral disgust may be different emotional constructs.<ref name=":0" />

Studies have found that disgust has been known to predict prejudice and discrimination.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Liu |first1=Yunzhe |last2=Lin |first2=Wanjun |last3=Xu |first3=Pengfei |last4=Zhang |first4=Dandan |last5=Luo |first5=Yuejia |title=Neural basis of disgust perception in racial prejudice |journal=Human Brain Mapping |date=29 September 2015 |volume=36 |issue=12 |pages=5275–5286 |doi=10.1002/hbm.23010 |pmid=26417673 |pmc=6868979 }}</ref><ref name=":1">{{cite journal |last1=Vartanian |first1=Lenny R. |last2=Trewartha |first2=Tara |last3=Vanman |first3=Eric J. |title=Disgust predicts prejudice and discrimination toward individuals with obesity |journal=Journal of Applied Social Psychology |date=16 December 2015 |volume=46 |issue=6 |pages=369–375 |doi=10.1111/jasp.12370 }}</ref> Through passive viewing tasks and functional magnetic resonance researchers were able to provide direct evidence that the insula is largely involved in racially biased perception of facial disgust through two distinct neural pathways: amygdala and insula, both areas of the brain that deal with emotion processing.<ref name=":0" /> It was found that racial prejudice elicited disgusted facial expressions. Disgust can also predict prejudice and discrimination towards individuals with obesity.<ref name=":1" /> Vertanian, Trewartha and [[Eric Vanman|Vanman]] (2016) showed participants photos of obese targets and non-obese targets performing everyday activities. They found that, compared to non-obese people, obese targets elicited more disgust, more negative attitudes and stereotypes, and a greater desire for a social distance from participants.

Jones & Fitness (2008)<ref name="Jones Fitness Moral hypervigilance"/> coined the term "moral [[hypervigilance]]" to describe the phenomenon that individuals who are prone to physical disgust are also prone to moral disgust. The link between physical disgust and moral disgust can be seen in the United States where criminals are often referred to as "slime" or "scum" and criminal activity as "stinking" or being "fishy". Furthermore, people often try to block out the stimuli of morally repulsive images in much the same way that they would block out the stimuli of a physically repulsive image. When people see an image of abuse, rape, or murder, they often avert their gazes to inhibit the incoming visual stimuli from the photograph just like they would if they saw a decomposing body.{{fact|date=September 2024}}

Moral judgments can be traditionally defined or thought of as directed by standards such as impartiality and respect towards others for their well-being. From more recent theoretical and empirical information, it can be suggested that [[morality]] may be guided by basic affective processes. [[Jonathan Haidt]] proposed that one's instant judgments about morality are experienced as a "flash of intuition" and that these affective perceptions operate rapidly, associatively, and outside of [[consciousness]].<ref name="David2011">{{cite journal |last1=David |first1=Bieke |last2=Olatunji |first2=Bunmi O. |title=The effect of disgust conditioning and disgust sensitivity on appraisals of moral transgressions |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |date=May 2011 |volume=50 |issue=7 |pages=1142–1146 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2011.02.004 }}</ref> From this, moral intuitions are believed to be stimulated prior to conscious moral cognitions which correlates with having a greater influence on moral judgments.<ref name="David2011" />

Research suggests that the experience of disgust can alter moral judgments. Many studies have focused on the average change in behavior across participants, with some studies indicating disgust stimuli intensifies the severity of moral judgments.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Schnall |first1=Simone |last2=Haidt |first2=Jonathan |last3=Clore |first3=Gerald L. |last4=Jordan |first4=Alexander H. |title=Disgust as Embodied Moral Judgment |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |date=9 May 2008 |volume=34 |issue=8 |pages=1096–1109 |doi=10.1177/0146167208317771 |pmid=18505801 |pmc=2562923 }}</ref> Later studies found the reverse effect,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Cummins |first1=Denise Dellarosa |last2=Cummins |first2=Robert C. |title=Emotion and Deliberative Reasoning in Moral Judgment |journal=Frontiers in Psychology |date=2012 |volume=3 |page=328 |doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2012.00328 |pmid=22973255 |pmc=3433709 |doi-access=free }}</ref> and some studies have suggested that the average effect of disgust on moral judgments is small or absent.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=May |first1=Joshua |title=Does Disgust Influence Moral Judgment? |journal=Australasian Journal of Philosophy |date=3 May 2013 |volume=92 |issue=1 |pages=125–141 |doi=10.1080/00048402.2013.797476 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Landy |first1=Justin F. |last2=Goodwin |first2=Geoffrey P. |title=Does Incidental Disgust Amplify Moral Judgment? A Meta-Analytic Review of Experimental Evidence |journal=Perspectives on Psychological Science |date=July 2015 |volume=10 |issue=4 |pages=518–536 |doi=10.1177/1745691615583128 |pmid=26177951 }}</ref><ref name="Ghelfi2020">{{cite journal |last1=Ghelfi |first1=Eric |last2=Christopherson |first2=Cody D. |last3=Urry |first3=Heather L. |last4=Lenne |first4=Richie L. |last5=Legate |first5=Nicole |last6=Ann Fischer |first6=Mary |last7=Wagemans |first7=Fieke M. A. |last8=Wiggins |first8=Brady |last9=Barrett |first9=Tamara |last10=Bornstein |first10=Michelle |last11=de Haan |first11=Bianca |last12=Guberman |first12=Joshua |last13=Issa |first13=Nada |last14=Kim |first14=Joan |last15=Na |first15=Elim |last16=O'Brien |first16=Justin |last17=Paulk |first17=Aidan |last18=Peck |first18=Tayler |last19=Sashihara |first19=Marissa |last20=Sheelar |first20=Karen |last21=Song |first21=Justin |last22=Steinberg |first22=Hannah |last23=Sullivan |first23=Dasan |title=Reexamining the Effect of Gustatory Disgust on Moral Judgment: A Multilab Direct Replication of Eskine, Kacinik, and Prinz (2011) |journal=Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science |date=March 2020 |volume=3 |issue=1 |pages=3–23 |doi=10.1177/2515245919881152 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Potentially reconciling these effects, one study indicated that the direction and size of the effect of disgust stimuli on moral judgment depends upon an individual's sensitivity to disgust.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Ong|first1=HH|title=Moral judgment modulation by disgust is bi-directionally moderated by individual sensitivity|journal=Frontiers in Psychology|date=2014|volume=5|pmc=3944793|pmid=24639665|doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2014.00194|page=194|doi-access=free }}</ref> One effort to reconcile the inconsistent findings suggests that studying the effects of induced disgust on moral judgments alone is insufficient. Instead, the magnitude of experienced disgust appears to be a critical factor. Research by Białek et al.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Białek|first1=Michał|last2=Muda|first2=Rafał|last3=Fugelsang|first3=Jonathan|last4=Friedman|first4=Ori|title=Disgust and Moral Judgment: Distinguishing Between Elicitors and Feelings Matters|journal=Social Psychological and Personality Science|year=2021|volume=12|issue=3|pages=304–313|doi=10.1177/1948550620919569}}</ref> found that self-reported levels of disgust were more predictive of changes in moral judgments than the mere presence of disgust elicitors. This approach may provide a more nuanced understanding of how disgust influences moral decision-making.

The effect also seems to be limited to a certain aspect of morality. Horberg et al. found that disgust plays a role in the development and intensification of moral judgments of purity in particular.<ref name="Horberg Oveis Keltner Cohen 2009">{{cite journal |last1=Horberg |first1=E. J. |last2=Oveis |first2=Christopher |last3=Keltner |first3=Dacher |last4=Cohen |first4=Adam B. |title=Disgust and the moralization of purity |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |date=2009 |volume=97 |issue=6 |pages=963–976 |doi=10.1037/a0017423 |pmid=19968413 }}</ref>  In other words, the feeling of disgust is often associated with a feeling that some image of what is pure has been violated. For example, a vegetarian might feel disgust after seeing another person eating meat because he/she has a view of vegetarianism as the pure state-of-being. When this state-of-being is violated, the vegetarian feels disgust. Furthermore, disgust appears to be uniquely associated with purity judgments, not with what is just/unjust or what is harmful/caregiving, while other emotions such as fear, anger, and sadness are "unrelated to moral judgments of purity".<ref name="Horberg Oveis Keltner Cohen 2009"/>

Some other research suggests that an individual's level of disgust sensitivity is due to their particular experience of disgust.<ref name="David2011" /> One's disgust sensitivity can be either high or low. The higher one's disgust sensitivity is, the greater the tendency to make stricter moral judgments.<ref name="David2011" /> Disgust sensitivity can also relate to various aspects of moral values, which can have a negative or positive impact. For example, Disgust sensitivity is associated with moral hypervigilance, which means people who have higher disgust sensitivity are more likely to think that other people who are suspects of a crime are more guilty. They also associate them as being morally evil and criminal, thus endorsing them to harsher punishment in the setting of a court.{{fact|date=September 2024}}

Disgust is also theorized as an evaluative [[emotion]] that can control moral [[behavior]].<ref name="David2011" /> When one experiences disgust, this emotion might signal that certain behaviors, objects, or people are to be avoided in order to preserve their [[wikt:pure|purity]]. Research has established that when the idea or concept of cleanliness is made salient then people make less severe moral judgments of others.<ref name="David2011" /> From this particular finding, it can be suggested that this reduces the experience of disgust and the ensuing threat of psychological impurity diminishes the apparent severity of moral transgressions.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=David |first1=Bieke |last2=Olatunji |first2=Bunmi O. |title=The effect of disgust conditioning and disgust sensitivity on appraisals of moral transgressions |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |date=May 2011 |volume=50 |issue=7 |pages=1142–1146 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2011.02.004 }}</ref>

===Political orientation===
In one study, people of differing political persuasions were shown disgusting images in a [[Functional magnetic resonance imaging|brain scanner]]. In [[Conservatism in the United States|conservatives]], the basal ganglia and amygdala and several other regions showed increased activity, while in [[Modern liberalism in the United States|liberals]] other regions of the brain increased in activity. Both groups reported similar conscious reactions to the images. The difference in activity patterns was large: the reaction to a single image could predict a person's political leanings with 95% accuracy.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ahn |first1=Woo-Young |last2=Kishida |first2=Kenneth T. |last3=Gu |first3=Xiaosi |last4=Lohrenz |first4=Terry |last5=Harvey |first5=Ann |last6=Alford |first6=John R. |last7=Smith |first7=Kevin B. |last8=Yaffe |first8=Gideon |last9=Hibbing |first9=John R. |last10=Dayan |first10=Peter |last11=Montague |first11=P. Read |title=Nonpolitical Images Evoke Neural Predictors of Political Ideology |journal=Current Biology |date=November 2014 |volume=24 |issue=22 |pages=2693–2699 |doi=10.1016/j.cub.2014.09.050 |pmid=25447997 |pmc=4245707 |bibcode=2014CBio...24.2693A }}</ref><ref>{{cite news |last1=Jones |first1=Dan |title=Left or right-wing? Brain's disgust response tells all |url=https://www.newscientist.com/article/dn26481-left-or-right-wing-brains-disgust-response-tells-all/ |work=New Scientist |date=30 October 2014 }}</ref> Later, however, such results have been proven to be mixed, with failed replications and questions about what is actually being measured also raising questions about the generalizability of the findings.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Smith |first1=Kevin B |last2=Warren |first2=Clarisse |date=2020 |title=Physiology predicts ideology. Or does it? The current state of political psychophysiology research |journal=Current Opinion in Behavioral Sciences |series=Political Ideologies |volume=34 |pages=88–93 |doi=10.1016/j.cobeha.2020.01.001 |s2cid=211040475 }}</ref>

===Self-disgust===
Although limited research has been done on self-disgust, one study found that self-disgust and severity of moral judgments were negatively correlated.<ref name="Olatunji David Ciesielski Who am I to judge?">{{cite journal |last1=Olatunji |first1=Bunmi O. |last2=David |first2=Bieke |last3=Ciesielski |first3=Bethany G. |title=Who am I to judge? Self-disgust predicts less punishment of severe transgressions |journal=Emotion |date=2012 |volume=12 |issue=1 |pages=169–173 |doi=10.1037/a0024074 |pmid=21707158 }}</ref> This is in contrast to findings related to disgust, which typically results in harsher judgments of transgressions. This implies that disgust directed towards the self functions very differently from disgust directed towards other people or objects.<ref name="Olatunji David Ciesielski Who am I to judge?"/> Self-disgust "may reflect a pervasive condition of self-loathing that makes it difficult to assign deserving punishment to others".<ref name="Olatunji David Ciesielski Who am I to judge?"/> In other words, those who feel self-disgust cannot easily condemn others to punishment because they feel that they may also be deserving of punishment. The concept of self-disgust has been implicated in several mental health conditions, including depression,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Overton |first1=P. G. |last2=Markland |first2=F. E. |last3=Taggart |first3=H. S. |last4=Bagshaw |first4=G. L. |last5=Simpson |first5=J. |title=Self-disgust mediates the relationship between dysfunctional cognitions and depressive symptomatology |journal=Emotion |date=2008 |volume=8 |issue=3 |pages=379–385 |doi=10.1037/1528-3542.8.3.379 |pmid=18540753 }}</ref> obsessive-compulsive disorder<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Olatunji |first1=Bunmi O. |last2=Cox |first2=Rebecca |last3=Kim |first3=Eun Ha |title=Self-Disgust Mediates the Associations Between Shame and Symptoms of Bulimia and Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder |journal=Journal of Social and Clinical Psychology |date=March 2015 |volume=34 |issue=3 |pages=239–258 |doi=10.1521/jscp.2015.34.3.239 }}</ref> and eating disorders.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Bektas |first1=Sevgi |last2=Keeler |first2=Johanna Louise |last3=Anderson |first3=Lisa M. |last4=Mutwalli |first4=Hiba |last5=Himmerich |first5=Hubertus |last6=Treasure |first6=Janet |date=2022 |title=Disgust and Self-Disgust in Eating Disorders: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis |journal=Nutrients |volume=14 |issue=9 |page=1728 |doi=10.3390/nu14091728 |pmc=9102838 |pmid=35565699 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

==Functions==
{{One source|section|date=February 2018}}
The emotion of disgust can be described to serve as an effective mechanism following occurrences of negative social value, provoking repulsion, and desire for social distance.<ref name="Sherman2011">{{cite journal |last1=Sherman |first1=Gary D. |last2=Haidt |first2=Jonathan |title=Cuteness and Disgust: The Humanizing and Dehumanizing Effects of Emotion |journal=Emotion Review |date=28 June 2011 |volume=3 |issue=3 |pages=245–251 |doi=10.1177/1754073911402396 }}</ref> The origin of disgust can be defined by motivating the avoidance of offensive things, and in the context of a [[social environment]], it can become an instrument of social avoidance.<ref name="Sherman2011" /> An example of disgust in action can be found from the [[Bible]] in the book of [[Book of Leviticus|Leviticus]] (See especially Leviticus chapter 11). Leviticus includes direct commandments from [[God]] to avoid disgust causing individuals, which included people who were sexually immoral and those who had [[leprosy]].<ref name="Sherman2011" /> Disgust is known to promote the avoidance of [[Pathogenic bacteria|pathogens]] and [[disease]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kupfer |first1=Tom R. |last2=Giner-Sorolla |first2=Roger |title=Communicating Moral Motives |journal=Social Psychological and Personality Science |date=15 December 2016 |volume=8 |issue=6 |pages=632–640 |doi=10.1177/1948550616679236 |url=https://kar.kent.ac.uk/59822/1/SPPS.Social%20signal%20author%20final.pdf }}</ref>

As an effective instrument for reducing motivations for [[social interaction]], disgust can be anticipated to interfere with [[dehumanization]] or the maltreatment of persons as less than human.<ref name="Sherman2011" /> Research was performed which conducted several functional magnetic resonance images (fMRI) in which participants viewed images of individuals from stigmatized groups that were associated with disgust, which were drug addicts and homeless people.<ref name="Sherman2011" /> What the study found was that people were not inclined in making inferences about the mental conditions of these particular disgust inducing groups.<ref name="Sherman2011" /> Therefore, examining images of homeless people and drug addicts caused disgust in the response of the people who participated with this study.<ref name="Sherman2011" /> This study coincides with disgust following the [[law of contagion]], which explains that contact with disgusting material renders one disgusting.<ref name="Sherman2011" /> Disgust can be applied towards people and can function as maltreatment towards another human being. Disgust can exclude people from being a part of a [[clique]] by leading to the view that they are merely less than human. An example of this is if groups were to avoid people from outside of their own particular group. Some researchers have distinguished between two different forms of dehumanization. The first form is the denial of uniquely human traits, examples include: products of culture and modification.<ref name="Sherman2011" /> The second form is the denial of [[human nature]], examples include: [[emotionality]] and [[personality]].<ref name="Sherman2011" />

Failure to attribute distinctively human traits to a group leads to [[animalistic dehumanization]], which defines the object group or individual as savage, [[:wikt:crude|crude]], and similar to [[animals]].<ref name="Sherman2011" /> These forms of dehumanization have clear connections to disgust.<ref name="Sherman2011" /> Researchers have proposed that many disgust elicitors are disgusting because they are reminders that humans are not diverse from other creatures.<ref name="Sherman2011" /> With the aid of disgust, animalistic dehumanization directly reduces one's moral concerns towards excluding members from the outer group.<ref name="Sherman2011" /> Disgust can be a cause and consequence of dehumanization.<ref name="Sherman2011" /> Animalistic dehumanization may generate feelings of disgust and revulsion.<ref name="Sherman2011" /> Feelings of disgust, through rousing [[social distance]], may lead to dehumanization. Therefore, a person or group that is generally connected with disgusting effects and seen as physically unclean may induce moral avoidance.<ref name="Sherman2011" /> Being deemed disgusting produces a variety of cognitive effects that result in [[:wikt:exclusion|exclusion]] from the perceived inner group.<ref name="Sherman2011" />

==Political and legal aspects of disgust==
{{Refimprove|section|date=September 2024}}
The emotion disgust has been noted to feature strongly in the public sphere in relation to issues and debates, among other things, regarding [[anatomy]], [[sex]] and [[bioethics]]. There is a range of views by different commentators on the role, purpose and effects of disgust on public discourse.

[[Leon Kass]], a [[bioethicist]], has advocated that "in crucial cases...repugnance is the emotional expression of deep wisdom, beyond reason's power fully to articulate it." in relation to bio-ethical issues (See: [[Wisdom of repugnance]]).

[[Martha Nussbaum]], a [[jurist]] and [[ethicist]], explicitly rejects disgust as an appropriate guide for legislating, arguing the "politics of disgust" is an unreliable emotional reaction with no inherent wisdom. Furthermore, she argues this "politics of disgust" has in the past and present had the effects of supporting bigotry in the forms of sexism, racism and antisemitism and links the emotion of disgust to support for laws against [[miscegenation]] and the oppressive caste system in India.  In place of this "politics of disgust", Nussbaum argues for the [[harm principle]] from [[John Stuart Mill]] as the proper basis for legislating. Nussbaum argues the harm principle supports the legal ideas of [[consent]], the [[age of majority]] and [[privacy]] and protects citizens. She contrasts this with the "politics of disgust" which she argues denies citizens humanity and equality before the law on no rational grounds and cause palpable social harm. (See Martha Nussbaum, ''From Disgust to Humanity: Sexual Orientation and Constitutional Law''). Nussbaum published ''Hiding From Humanity: Disgust, Shame, and the Law'' in 2004; the book examines the relationship of disgust and [[shame]] to a society's laws.  Nussbaum identifies disgust as a marker that bigoted, and often merely majoritarian, discourse employs to "place", by diminishment and denigration, a despised minority.  Removing "disgust" from public discourse constitutes an important step in achieving humane and tolerant democracies.

[[Leigh Turner]] (2004) has argued that "reactions of disgust are often built upon prejudices that should be challenged and rebutted."  On the other hand, writers, such as Kass, find wisdom in adhering to one's initial feelings of disgust.  A number of writers{{who|date=April 2019}} on the theory of disgust find it to be the proto-legal foundation of human law.

Disgust has also figured prominently in the work of several other philosophers.  [[Nietzsche]] became disgusted with the music and orientation of [[Richard Wagner]], as well as other aspects of 19th century culture and morality. [[Jean-Paul Sartre]] wrote widely about experiences involving various [[negative emotion]]s related to disgust.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Sartre |first1=Jean-Paul |translator-last1=Barnes |translator-first1=Hazel Estella |translator-link1=Hazel Barnes |title=Being and Nothingness |date=1992 |publisher=Simon and Schuster |isbn=978-0-671-86780-5 |pages=604–607 |title-link=Being and Nothingness }}</ref>

== ''The Hydra's Tale: Imagining Disgust'' ==
According to the book ''The Hydra's Tale: Imagining Disgust'' by Robert Rawdon Wilson,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Wilson |first1=Robert |title=On Disgust: A Menippean Interview. Interview with Robert Wilson |journal=Canadian Review of Comparative Literature/ Revue Canadienne de Littérature Comparée |date=2007 |volume=34 |issue=2 |url=https://ejournals.library.ualberta.ca/index.php/crcl/article/view/10712 }}</ref> disgust may be further subdivided into physical disgust, associated with physical or metaphorical uncleanliness, and moral disgust, a similar feeling related to courses of action. For example; "I am disgusted by the hurtful things that you are saying."  Moral disgust should be understood as [[culturally determined]]; physical disgust as more universally grounded. The book also discusses moral disgust as an aspect of the representation of disgust.  Wilson does this in two ways. First, he discusses representations of disgust in literature, film and fine art.  Since there are characteristic facial expressions (the clenched nostrils, the pursed lips)—as [[Charles Darwin]], [[Paul Ekman]], and others have shown—they may be represented with more or less skill in any set of circumstances imaginable. There may even be "disgust worlds" in which disgust motifs so dominate that it may seem that entire represented world is, in itself, disgusting. Second, since people know what disgust is as a primary, or visceral, emotion (with characteristic gestures and expressions), they may imitate it. Thus, Wilson argues that, for example, contempt is acted out on the basis of the visceral emotion, disgust, but is not identical with disgust. It is a "compound affect" that entails intellectual preparation, or formatting, and theatrical techniques. Wilson argues that there are many such "intellectual" compound affects—such as nostalgia and outrage—but that disgust is a fundamental and unmistakable example. Moral disgust, then, is different from visceral disgust; it is more conscious and more layered in performance.

Wilson links [[shame]] and [[guilt (emotion)|guilt]] to disgust (now transformed, wholly or partially, into self-disgust) primarily as a consequence rooted in [[self-consciousness]]. Referring to a passage in [[Doris Lessing]]'s ''The Golden Notebook'', Wilson writes that "the dance between disgust and shame takes place. A slow choreography unfolds before the mind's-eye."{{sfn|Wilson|2002|p=281}}

Wilson examines the claims of several jurists and legal scholars—such as William Ian Miller—that disgust must underlie positive law. "In the absence of disgust", he observes, stating their claim, ". . . there would be either total barbarism or a society ruled solely by force, violence and terror." The moral-legal argument, he remarks, "leaves much out of account."{{sfn|Wilson|2002|pp=51–52}} His own argument turns largely upon the human capacity to learn how to control, even to suppress, strong and problematic affects and, over time, for entire populations to abandon specific disgust responses.

==Plutchik's Wheel of Emotions==
{{multiple image
|align     = left
|direction = horizontal 
|image1    = 
|image2    = 
|image3    = 
|width     = 300px
|caption1  = Disgust combined with other primary emotions.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.adliterate.com/archives/Plutchik.emotion.theorie.POSTER.pdf |title=Robert Plutchik's Psychoevolutionary Theory of Basic Emotions |website=Adliterate.com |access-date=2017-06-05}}</ref><ref name="Turner2000">{{cite book|author=Jonathan Turner|title=On the Origins of Human Emotions: A Sociological Inquiry Into the Evolution of Human Affect|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=aEeSmDRsXkcC&pg=PA76|date=1 June 2000|publisher=Stanford University Press|isbn=978-0-8047-6436-0|page=76}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|title=A Fuzzy Inference System for Synergy Estimation of Simultaneous Emotion Dynamics in Agents|journal=International Journal of Scientific & Engineering Research|volume=2|issue=6|date=June 2011|url=http://www.ijser.org/paper/A_Fuzzy_Inference_System_for_Synergy_Estimation_of_Simultaneous_Emotion_Dynamics_in_Agents.html|author1=Atifa Athar|author2=M. Saleem Khan|author3=Khalil Ahmed|author4=Aiesha Ahmed|author5=Nida Anwar}}
</ref>
}}
Disgust is the opposite of trust on the [[Emotion classification#Plutchik's wheel of emotions|emotion wheel]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Plutchik |first1=Robert |title=The Emotions |date=1991 |publisher=University Press of America |isbn=978-0-8191-8286-9 |page=110 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JaQauznPoiEC&pg=PA110 }}</ref> A mild form of disgust is [[boredom]], while a more intense version is [[hatred|loathing]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Plutchik |first1=Robert |title=The Nature of Emotions |journal=American Scientist |date=2001 |volume=89 |issue=4 |pages=344–350 |doi=10.1511/2001.28.344 }}</ref>
